event_blocks.summary: The 2005 Champions League Final between AC Milan and Liverpool, held on May 25, 2005, at the Ataturk Olympic Stadium, is introduced with a grainy, monochrome montage of soccer highlights, featuring player celebrations and goalkeeper action, accompanied by static, crackling sounds, and digital distortion. An Italian commentator exclaims, "Milan è il campione d'Italia! Andriy! Would you believe it? A headshot! What a headshot!", while an English commentator notes, "Andriy Shevchenko, he must score. The weight of the world on his shoulders." A narrator explains that this final is part of a larger story, with both teams on the verge of lifting the Champions League trophy. As suspenseful electronic music begins, the scene shifts to color, showing Liverpool goalkeeper Jerzy Dudek in his black jersey. A Milan player, number 7 in white, takes a penalty shot, which Dudek dramatically saves by diving to his right, the save replayed in slow motion from behind the net. The sequence includes a close-up of a Liverpool player in red before transitioning to 'balon PRESENTS' and 'context' title cards, then fading to black.

event_blocks.summary: Following their 1990 league title win, Liverpool Football Club entered a transitional period, as indicated by a vintage title card reading 'LIVERPOOL'S PATH' and a narrator's observation that the club was 'still grasping on to the past,' visually supported by a 1990 team photo of Liverpool players in red 'Candy' jerseys with a trophy. Dynamic match footage showed a player in red celebrating a goal, leading to a commentator exclaiming, "Liverpool are European Champions!" as a player lifted a large silver trophy amidst cheering fans. The narrator then discussed the 1992 Premier League formation, noting Liverpool's continued questioning of future success, contrasted by a Sky Sports advertisement proclaiming 'It’s a whole new ball game.' Ex-Reds legend Graeme Souness was introduced as manager, implementing new training and diet methods, depicted by newspaper headlines like 'SOUNESS LANDS KOP JOB' and 'SOUNESS CRISIS: FANS WANT HIM GONE,' alongside footage of Souness reacting emotionally during a match. The narrator concluded that "three seasons of this experiment were enough," with the Reds finishing in sixth place, a sentiment reinforced by a timeline graphic displaying Liverpool’s league finishes under Souness (6th, 8th, 4th) from 92/93 to 94/95, featuring images of Souness, a player in red, and a goalkeeper in purple.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a close-up of two men in dark jackets discussing managerial tasks, set against a melancholic orchestral score, as a narrator contrasts the approaches of Roy Evans and a new manager, despite a strong start to an "experiment." Squad member David Thompson expresses confusion over leadership, with his quote appearing on screen. The "experiment" concluded with Roy Evans's resignation in November 1998, paving the way for Gérard Houllier, but the team was already out of contention for tournaments. At a November 1998 press conference, Evans announced his resignation, flanked by suited men. Fans holding Liverpool scarves showed mixed emotions, while a man in a dark jacket with glasses appeared concerned. Houllier implemented significant changes to strengthen discipline, removing players like captain Paul Ince, who is then introduced with his name displayed. Ex-legend Phil Thompson was brought in as assistant manager to instill discipline, also being introduced. A distressed man covering his face preceded a newspaper headline "HOW THE MIGHTY HAVE FALLEN!" and a league table showing Liverpool with 54 points, significantly behind United (79) and Arsenal (78). The narrator notes Houllier faced a tough job, finishing his first year in seventh place, 22 points behind champions United. The segment then shifts to the arrival of a new manager, given ample time, accompanied by a serious orchestral score. Gérard Houllier focused on developing youngsters like Michael Owen, Steven Gerrard, and Jamie Carragher, shown in a montage. Transfer fee graphics displayed for Hyypia (€3.90M), Henchoz (€5.40M), and Hamann (€12.00M) as Houllier strengthened the defense. Henchoz in a red jersey, a close-up of Patrick Berger, and a graphic of Heskey and Berger were shown, highlighting their "great performances," with Emile Heskey identified as a "new January signing." A 99/00 Premier League table confirmed Liverpool in fourth place with 67 points, qualifying for the UEFA Cup. The manager, in a white Reebok jacket, addressed players, followed by a match highlight of a Liverpool player scoring, symbolizing the "revolution" and "exciting seasons." Visually, Liverpool’s Fowler (number 9) scores, followed by a penalty save from the Liverpool Goalkeeper, and a dejected Liverpool player. Excited English commentary shouts "Candy Johnson and that's the one!" as a Birmingham City player (Johnson, number 19) is seen, confirming a penalty goal amidst crowd cheers. Arsenal’s David Seaman makes a save, and a Liverpool player reacts with frustration, while Graeme Souness watches concerned. An 'ARSENAL F.C. Double Winners 1997/98' banner is visible. Intense English commentary exclaims "Owen has won the cup for Liverpool! All by himself!" as a Liverpool player in red celebrates, confirming Michael Owen's winning goal amidst roaring crowd. The narrative continues with the 2001 UEFA Cup Final against Alavés on May 16, 2001, which the narrator describes as a 'crazy match' after Liverpool's journey past Roma, Porto, and Barcelona. Visually, Liverpool’s Babbel scores in the 4th minute, followed by goals from Alonso (27'), McAllister (41'), and Fowler (73'). Excited English commentary highlights a "chance for Steven Gerrard" and another "real chance that is clearly taken," followed by "That's a fine goal!" Spanish commentary then excitedly announces "Javi Moreno! Gol! Gol! Ha marcado Javi Moreno!" as an Alavés player is seen, confirming their goal. event_blocks.summary: The 2001 UEFA Cup Final saw Liverpool secure a dramatic 5-4 victory over Alavés with an own goal in extra time, a triumph that contributed to their "unique treble" and marked Steven Gerrard's emergence as PFA Young Player of the Year. Following a montage of past successes, including the 1990 league title, the narrative shifted to the "Summer of 2001" and the ambition to win the elusive Premier League, juxtaposed with a flashback to the 1985 Heysel Stadium tragedy, which had prevented Champions League qualification until this period. The departure of Robbie Fowler to Leeds United, signaled by a newspaper clipping, coincided with significant transfer activity, including the acquisition of Jerzy Dudek and Nicolas Anelka, though the team compensated well in the transfer windows. Manager Gérard Houllier faced immense pressure, suffering a health crisis during a match that led to his hospitalization and a five-month absence, during which Phil Thompson managed the team. Houllier's surprise return for a crucial Champions League match against Roma on March 19, 2002, culminated in a 2-0 victory and a quarter-final berth, fueling his declaration, 'WE ARE 10 GAMES FROM GREATNESS.' However, this European journey ended with a 4-2 loss to Leverkusen in the quarter-finals, and the team finished second in the league behind Arsenal. A failure to make Anelka's loan permanent led to his transfer to Chelsea and the signings of El Hadji Diouf and Salif Diao, but the team's decline continued, marked by crashing out of the Champions League group stages against Valencia. The "02/03 Premier League" season concluded with a 2-1 loss to Chelsea on May 11, 2003, ending their Champions League aspirations for the following season and leading to headlines questioning Houllier's leadership.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a tense moment from the 2001 UEFA Cup Final, showing Liverpool players in red and Alavés players in blue and white, as a narrator describes the match as a 4-4 draw sealed by a golden goal scored inadvertently by an Alavés defender. The celebration continues with players hoisting the UEFA Cup trophy, transitioning to a formal ceremony where a man in a tuxedo presents the trophy to a young player, as the narrator highlights Liverpool's "unique treble" and introduces "young Steven Gerrard" as a new champion who won the PFA Young Player of the Year award. The scene shifts to a montage of Liverpool’s past glories, including a man in a suit posing with multiple trophies and a graphic of the Premier League trophy with '90' PREMIER LEAGUE CHAMPIONS' text. A black screen with 'SUMMER 2001' appears, followed by a close-up of a man’s intense, contemplative face, while the narrator explains that after years of drought, Liverpool won several titles in a single season, and it was finally the time to win the elusive Premier League title. Dramatic, melancholic instrumental music begins to play as a historical flash of '1985' appears over a chaotic scene of police and fans, evoking the Heysel Stadium tragedy, which the narrator explicitly references as the "Heysel disaster" from 1985, noting the Reds' qualification for the Champions League for the first time since then. A huddle of Liverpool players in red and green kits is shown, followed by the final frames of a man holding a white Leeds United jersey with 'FOWLER 27' on the back, standing beside a man in a red Liverpool jersey, symbolizing a transfer or legacy moment, as the narrator discusses big ambitions for the upcoming season and mentions "the Reds let go of a club..." before the audio cuts off.

The segment opens with a black-and-white newspaper clipping declaring 'Fowler’s days at Liverpool numbered,' circled in red, as a narrator explains Robbie Fowler's transfer to Leeds United due to a strained relationship with his French manager. This is followed by a red grid graphic detailing transfer fees for players like Jerzy Dudek, Milan Baroš, Nicolas Anelka, and Steve Riese, aligning with the narrator's comment that the team compensated well in the transfer windows. The scene then shifts to a tense moment from a 2001/02 Premier League match, showing Liverpool players in red 'Carlsberg' kits in a tunnel, and a pensive Gérard Houllier, the manager, with his hand on his chin. The narrator reveals the 'French manager' faced mounting pressures and was rushed to Broadgreen Hospital at half-time during a Leeds match in October due to serious heart issues, a somber visual reinforced by a nighttime street scene with a car passing the hospital sign and a fan in a red Liverpool jersey with a head bandage. After assistant manager Phil Thompson took over for five months, the 'Frenchman' made a surprise return for a crucial Champions League match against Italian champions Roma on March 19, 2002. Players in white 'INA Assitalia' kits enter the pitch, and Houllier is seen clapping and gesturing on the sidelines. A commentator exclaims, 'A surprise homecoming for Gérard Houllier!' amidst crowd cheers. Match highlights show Heskey scoring in the 64th minute, securing a 2-0 victory for Liverpool, which the narrator confirms led them to the quarter-finals. The video concludes with Houllier's quote, 'WE ARE 10 GAMES FROM GREATNESS,' displayed over a red grid, followed by him walking through a stadium tunnel to applause, and a celebratory image of a Liverpool player, likely Heskey, raising his arm in triumph, as the narrator reflects on that 'beautiful European night against Roma' as a beginning.

The segment opens with a Liverpool player in a red jersey, possibly Heskey, celebrating passionately with a raised fist, embracing a teammate whose back shows 'LIMANEN' and number '37'. This celebratory visual is quickly juxtaposed with a somber narration, stating this period marked 'the beginning of the end for the reds and Houllier'. The European journey is shown ending with a wide shot of the '01/02 CHAMPIONS LEAGUE - QTR FINALS' match where Leverkusen defeated Liverpool 4(4-3)2, with Lucio scoring in the 84th minute, as the audio confirms their 'European journey stopped after the loss against Leverkusen'. The audio clarifies that Liverpool 'failed to make Nicola Anelka's loan a permanent deal' and 'replaced him with El Hadji Diouf and Salif Diao, the Senegalese duo'. A group photo with a man in a beige suit and players is shown, followed by Anelka in a blue Chelsea jersey, reinforcing his departure. The team's decline is further illustrated by a graphic of 'BENITEZ' and 'AIMAR' in a Valencia jersey, as the audio recounts Liverpool crashing out of the Champions League group stages against 'an exciting Valencia team led by the Spaniard, Rafa Benitez'. The final scenes show the '02/03 PREMIER LEAGUE - GW38 CHELSEA 2-1 LIVERPOOL MAY 11, 2003' match, with the audio confirming 'The Reds failed to win in their last faithful league match against Chelsea, which ended their chances of playing Champions League football next [season]', culminating in a stark newspaper headline 'TROUBLE AHEAD FOR CLUELESS HOULLIER' next to a black-and-white image of a Liverpool player.

event_blocks.summary: A black-and-white newspaper clipping with the headline 'TROUBLE AHEAD FOR CLUELESS HOULLIER' and a photo of Gérard Houllier sets a somber tone, with a narrator stating the League Cup final win against United was the "only bright spot of an average season," a sentiment contrasted by vibrant footage of a Liverpool player in red triumphantly lifting the trophy after the 2-0 victory on March 2, 2003. Controversy resurfaces with another headline, 'LIVERPOOL ARE BACKING HOULLIER,' as the narrator explains management stood by their manager despite fan tension, a situation Houllier himself seemed to address at a press conference, his expression serious, with a quote graphic revealing his suspicion that players were 'PLAYING BADLY TO GET RID OF ME' to force his dismissal. The narrative then shifts to a transfer graphic showing 'KEWELL' with a €10.00M fee from Leeds United, followed by the 03/04 League Table where Liverpool, despite acquiring the "Aussie star," sat significantly behind the top teams, struggling to challenge for the title, though they ultimately secured a Champions League spot. This performance, however, was insufficient to retain Houllier, who departed by mutual consent, possibly due to health reasons, concluding with a tense close-up of Houllier whose own voice, with a French accent, states, "If people decide, you know, that I'm not the right man for the right place, so what? I mean, I'm not important. I'm not here for myself. I'm here for the club," before fading to black with 'LIVERPOOL'S PATH — MILAN'S PATH'.

event_blocks.summary: The video opens with a close-up of a man’s face and the text 'MILAN'S PATH' accompanied by ominous, deep background music with a ticking sound, transitioning to a jubilant group photo of AC Milan players in red-and-black jerseys celebrating with trophies, as a narrator states that in the late 80s and early 90s, only Milan consistently appeared on podiums. The scene shifts to a dynamic pitch celebration where players, including one with long dreadlocks in white kits, hoist the Champions League trophy amid crowd chants of 'Il Milan! Forza Milan!'. A man in a blue tracksuit cycles through a green landscape as the narrator notes that under both Arrigo Sacchi and Fabio Capello, Milan achieved all possible European club honors. The narrative then shows a montage of men in suits, including one with sunglasses, in formal settings, with the narrator highlighting the first decade of Silvio Berlusconi's presidency as a 'huge success'. A newspaper clipping displays a headline 'Madrid ha già un diavolo per Capello' above a photo of two men in suits shaking hands, as the narrator explains that successful projects require transitions, and Milan's came after Capello's 1996 move to Madrid. Silvio Berlusconi, a man in a dark suit, smiles broadly, and a split-screen shows him beside the AC Milan crest and waving a 'FORZA ITALIA' flag, with the narrator detailing how the Rossoneri underwent significant changes that summer as Berlusconi shifted focus to politics, leading him to hand over the club. The segment concludes with a close-up of Silvio Berlusconi laughing heartily.

This transitions to a jubilant group photo of AC Milan players in their iconic red-and-black striped jerseys, celebrating with trophies, as a narrator explains that at the end of the 80s and beginning of the 90s, only one club, Milan, had its name repeated on several different podiums. The scene shifts to a dynamic celebration on the pitch, where players in white kits, including one with long dreadlocks, hoist the Champions League trophy amidst chanting crowd sounds of 'Il Milan! Forza Milan!'. A man in a blue tracksuit is then shown riding a bicycle through a green, rural landscape, while the narrator mentions that whether it was Arrigo Sacchi or Fabio Capello leading the team, Milan had achieved everything possible for a European club. The narrative returns to the past with a montage of men in suits, including one with a bald head and sunglasses, interacting in formal settings. The narrator highlights the first ten years for Silvio Berlusconi as president of AC Milan as a 'huge success'. A newspaper clipping appears, featuring a black-and-white photo of two men in suits shaking hands, with the headline 'Madrid ha già un diavolo per Capello'. The narrator explains that each successful project needs a transitional period of reshuffling assets and reviving the team, and for Milan, this period came after Capello's departure in '96 to Madrid. The video continues with Silvio Berlusconi, a man in a dark suit, smiling broadly. A split-screen then shows him in two poses: one beside the AC Milan crest and another waving a 'FORZA ITALIA' flag, as the narrator details how throughout the summer, the Rossoneri made numerous big changes as Berlusconi's focus shifted from Milan to his political plans and responsibilities, leading him to hand over his club. The segment concludes with a close-up of Silvio Berlusconi laughing heartily, reinforcing a sense of joy and legacy.

event_blocks.summary: Vice President Adriano Galliani adjusted his earpiece while General Manager Ariedo Braida was shown on a split-screen, as a narrator detailed the "trainwreck" beginnings under Galliani, characterized by a focus on transfer quantity over quality. A player in a red-and-black jersey scored a goal, immediately followed by a graphic listing new players such as Reiziger, Dugarry, Blomqvist, and Davids with their respective transfer fees, underscoring the transfer-centric approach. The scene shifted to a red grid background featuring Capello and Uruguayan manager Oscar Tabárez, whom the Rossoneri appointed after Capello, but the transfers "didn't gel quickly," leading to Tabárez's rapid dismissal. A newspaper clipping bearing the headline 'DISASTRO MILAN' amplified the crisis narrative, with the narrator explaining the club's attempt to "revive the glory days" by re-hiring Arrigo Sacchi, who had recently departed the Italian national team. A smiling Galliani was then seen with a man wearing a blue 'ITALIA' cap, preceding another goal. The narrator continued, asserting Sacchi "couldn't do much" and was "destined for failure," as the 96/97 Serie A league table materialized, revealing Milan in a dismal 11th position, marking the "worst showing in the Berlusconi era." This abysmal performance, the narrator explained, "drove club legends Tassotti and Baresi to finally retire," their names subsequently displayed prominently. The segment concluded with a black-and-white image of a bald man in a dark coat and sunglasses, gazing upwards, strongly implied to be Arrigo Sacchi contemplating the team's difficulties, as the narrator concluded that the club's goals and philosophy were "not clear." A consistent background music track permeated the entire segment.

event_blocks.summary: Following a period of failure under Capello, marked by a 5-0 loss to Roma and a 10th-place league finish in 1998, AC Milan sought a new direction with Alberto Zaccheroni, who was brought in from Udinese by Galliani and Braida after securing third place the previous season. Zaccheroni's arrival saw significant transfers, including top goal scorer Oliver Bierhoff and Danish wing-back Helveg, leading to a renewed spirit and strong competition throughout the 1998-99 season, culminating in a surprise Serie A title win, celebrated with shouts of 'È finita! Il Milan è campione d'Italia!' amidst jubilant staff. The subsequent seasons, however, brought challenges; despite improved market decisions with the signings of Andriy Shevchenko, Gennaro Gattuso, and Serginho in the summer of '99, Milan struggled in Europe, finishing last in their group and failing to pressure Lazio and Juventus in the 99/00 league, ending in third place. Increased pressure mounted on Zaccheroni with substantial spending on Kaladze and Redondo, while the 2001 Champions League Final was announced for the San Siro. After crashing out of the Champions League in March 2001, with results including a 2-0 loss to Galatasaray, Berlusconi fired Zaccheroni, appointing Cesare Maldini as caretaker. Despite a historic 6-0 Derby della Madonnina victory against Inter on May 11, 2001, the team failed to secure a Champions League spot, finishing the 00/01 Serie A season with Roma at 75 points.

A coach in a suit, identified as Capello, is seen animatedly gesturing on the sidelines, followed by a montage of Milan players in red-and-black kits, reflecting the team's struggles. The narrator confirms that 'transfers failed again,' and the 'script was repeated' as Milan lost heavily 5-0 to Roma on May 3, 1998, a defeat shown with on-screen text. A league table graphic then highlights Milan's 10th place, with the narrator stating the Rossoneri 'failed to capture a European spot' for the second year. A close-up of a young Massimo Ambrosini is overlaid with his quote, echoed by the narrator, that players 'were not motivated' and Capello 'had an extremely tough job.' This leads to the introduction of Alberto Zaccheroni, shown thoughtfully in a black 'Hyundai' jacket, as the narrator explains Galliani sought a 'new face' and brought Zaccheroni from Udinese after he 'successfully secured third position last season.' A narrator describes Zaccheroni's successful 3-4-3 strategy, highlighting crucial goals from Bierhoff and Leonardo, the runs of wing-back Guly, and the threat of George Weah, which led the Rossoneri to surprise everyone and win the title. The scene transitions to action footage from the 98/99 Serie A match between Perugia and Milan on May 22, 1999, showing Guly celebrating a goal with a raised arm and clenched fist, followed by a wide-angle view of the goal and the Perugia goalkeeper diving in vain. event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a commentator's excited exclamation of "Serginho! 6-0!" as AC Milan's Serginho, wearing number 27, is tackled by Inter Milan's Zanetti (#4) and Crespo (#24) during a decisive 6-0 victory in the '00/01 SERIE A - GW30', a result noted as historically unprecedented since 1929. Jubilant AC Milan fans are shown celebrating before the focus shifts to the sidelines where the "Turkish manager" Fatih Terim, clad in a dark suit, passionately argues, his intense expression captured in a close-up. A graphic introduces 'FATIH TERIM' alongside his contemplative photo, with the narrator explaining Terim's selection was based on his past success with Galatasaray and Galliani's desire for a manager with "great charisma." AC Milan's significant investment is then showcased with a group photo of Inzaghi (€36.15M), Rui Costa (€41.32M), and Pirlo (€17.04M) in training kits, identified as key signings. A graphic featuring a smiling Silvio Berlusconi highlights Milan's record transfer spending in 2001 at €145.13M, described as "the most expensive in Berlusconi's era," leading to "expectations through the roof." However, the situation deteriorates as Terim is seen being physically restrained on the pitch, followed by a newspaper headline 'TERIM LEAVES MILAN.' The narrator confirms Terim "was fired in November after many bad results and because of the language barrier," a sentiment echoed by Andrea Pirlo's quote graphic: 'THE TRANSLATOR HAD SOME PROBLEMS GETTING TERIM’S MESSAGE ACROSS.' The segment concludes with a serious Galliani on a phone call against a blurred stadium background, accompanied by somber orchestral music. The narrative then shifts to Galliani making a swift decision to contact Carlo Ancelotti, who was returning to Parma for a second stint. A split-screen graphic dated 'NOV 5, 2001' displays both Galliani and Ancelotti on phone calls, with the Parma logo above Ancelotti, accompanied by a distinct phone dialing sound. A quote from Galliani appears, reinforcing the narrator's statement about Ancelotti's promise to prioritize Milan if the 'Rossoneri' called him to coach. Ancelotti's past is then explored, showing him in a black jacket with the Juventus crest in 1999, celebrating an Intertoto Cup win with Del Piero. The narrator describes Ancelotti as a 'gamble' at the time, a manager who only won the Intertoto Cup with 'Juve' despite a star-studded team. The video cuts to Ancelotti in a suit, now with AC Milan players, including a young Kaka, against the AC Milan crest. The narrator notes he was joining a team that had invested heavily but failed to achieve anything yet. A league table for the 01/02 Serie A season highlights Milan's 55 points, trailing top teams, as Galliani reappears looking concerned. The narrator explains that Carlo didn't immediately change the season's course, needing time, but the Rossoneri still secured a Champions League spot for the next season, which was Berlusconi's 'number one priority.' The segment concludes with graphics detailing significant transfer fees: Nesta (€31.00M), Seedorf (€22.50M), and Rivaldo (FREE), with their images. The narrator confirms these investments, stating 'Alessandro Nesta' was brought from Lazio, 'Sidor' from Inter, and 'Rivaldo' on a free transfer, emphasizing the importance of these signings as a hand signs a document and Galliani looks down in reflection, all set to a continuous background music track.

The video showcases AC Milan's significant investment, displaying a group photo of Inzaghi (€36.15M), Rui Costa (€41.32M), and Pirlo (€17.04M) in training kits, all named by the narrator as key signings. A graphic featuring a smiling Silvio Berlusconi highlights Milan's record transfer spending in 2001 (€145.13M), described by the narrator as "the most expensive in Berlusconi's era," leading to "expectations through the roof." However, the narrative quickly turns as Terim is seen being physically restrained on the pitch, followed by a newspaper headline 'TERIM LEAVES MILAN'. The narrator confirms Terim "was fired in November after many bad results and because of the language barrier," a point reinforced by Andrea Pirlo's quote graphic: 'THE TRANSLATOR HAD SOME PROBLEMS GETTING TERIM’S MESSAGE ACROSS.' The segment concludes with a serious, focused Galliani on a phone call, against a blurred stadium background, as somber orchestral music plays.

The segment opens with a serious Galliani on a phone call against a blurred stadium, as a male narrator explains he made a quick decision to call Carlo Ancelotti, who was returning to Parma for a second stint. A split-screen graphic dated 'NOV 5, 2001' then shows both Galliani and Ancelotti on phone calls, with the Parma logo above Ancelotti, accompanied by a distinct phone dialing sound. A quote from Galliani appears, reinforcing the narrator's statement about Ancelotti's promise to prioritize Milan if the 'Rossoneri' called him to coach. The narrative shifts to Ancelotti's past, showing him in a black jacket with the Juventus crest in 1999, celebrating an Intertoto Cup win with Del Piero. The narrator describes Ancelotti as a 'gamble' at the time, a manager who only won the Intertoto Cup with 'Juve' despite a star-studded team. The video then cuts to Ancelotti in a suit, now with AC Milan players, including a young Kaka, against the AC Milan crest. The narrator notes he was coming to a team that had invested heavily but failed to achieve anything yet. A league table for the 01/02 Serie A season highlights Milan's 55 points, trailing top teams, as Galliani reappears looking concerned. The narrator explains that Carlo didn't immediately change the season's course, needing time, but the Rossoneri still secured a Champions League spot for the next season, which was Berlusconi's 'number one priority'. The segment concludes with graphics detailing significant transfer fees: Nesta (€31.00M), Seedorf (€22.50M), and Rivaldo (FREE), with their images. The narrator confirms these investments, stating 'Alessandro Nesta' was brought from Lazio, 'Sidor' from Inter, and 'Rivaldo' on a free transfer, emphasizing the importance of these signings as a hand signs a document and Galliani looks down in reflection, all set to a continuous background music track.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with the 'MILAN LAB' logo and a portrait of its founder, Belgian doctor Jean-Pierre Meersseman, establishing an analytical tone as the narrator explains the lab's 2000 inception post-Fernando Redondo's career-threatening injury, aiming to minimize risks and optimize player performance, with Redondo's €17.50M transfer fee highlighted. A montage follows, showcasing AC Milan players in red-and-black jerseys, including training drills and gym exercises, leading to Carlo Ancelotti, depicted with a serious expression and the AC Milan crest, discussing his strong player relationships and the pivotal 2002 decision to deploy Andrea Pirlo as a deep-lying midfielder, a tactical shift illustrated by a graphic featuring Gattuso, Pirlo, Rui Costa, and Seedorf, noting Pirlo's previous limited opportunities as an advanced playmaker. Pirlo's agreement to this role, recalling its use by Carlo Mazzone at Brescia, is confirmed through match footage of a free-kick and a dialogue exchange where Ancelotti asks about stealing the ball and initiating play, and Pirlo responds affirmatively. The segment then shifts to the 2002/03 Champions League, detailing Milan's 0-4 away victory against Deportivo La Coruña at the Riazor Stadium, with a 4-3-2-1 formation graphic showing Rui Costa and Rivaldo behind striker Inzaghi, despite inconsistent league form and Andriy Shevchenko's slow return from injury, as evidenced by a newspaper clipping about his knee injury. Milan's progression to the Champions League final is highlighted through quarter-final footage against Ajax (3-2) and a dramatic semi-final draw against Inter (1-1) where Shevchenko scores a stoppage-time equalizer, accompanied by crowd cheers and commentator exclamations. The upcoming final against Juventus is framed as an 'Italian taste' match, with headlines noting Ancelotti facing his former club and Alessandro Del Piero opposing him, further contextualized by a Juventus 4-4-2 formation graphic featuring Buffon, Zambrotta, Del Piero, and David Trezeguet, underscoring Ancelotti's managerial history.

raw_segments: The video opens with a red grid background featuring the 'MILAN LAB' logo and a portrait of Jean-Pierre Meersseman, identified by a narrator as the Belgian doctor and founder of Milan Lab. This sets a formal, analytical tone, as the narrator explains that the lab was established after Fernando Redondo's injury in 2000, which made him miss two years of football, prompting management to decrease injury risks and help players perform their best. A graphic highlights Redondo’s transfer fee of €17.50M. The scene transitions to a montage of AC Milan players in their iconic red-and-black jerseys, including close-ups of a player in a red training top and another in a black-and-red jersey, followed by a player walking on a training ground and another performing leg exercises in a gym. A man in a red tracksuit with a headband speaks animatedly, then a close-up shows Carlo Ancelotti with a serious expression. The narrator details how Ancelotti, shown in a black jacket with the AC Milan crest, created strong player relationships and made the important decision to play Andrea Pirlo as a deep-lying midfielder, a move suggested in August 2002. This is illustrated with a tactical formation graphic featuring Gattuso, Pirlo, Rui Costa, and Seedorf, as the narrator explains that Pirlo previously had minimal chances when Rui Costa started, and was considered an advanced playmaker. The video cuts to match footage of Pirlo (number 21) executing a free-kick. Ancelotti's quote asks: 'IF I ASKED YOU TO STEAL THE BALL FROM THE OPPONENT AND START THE PLAY... WHAT WOULD YOU SAY?', to which Andrea Pirlo responds: 'I ALREADY DID IT WITH MAZZONE IN BRESCIA.' The narrator confirms Pirlo's agreement to the change, citing Carlo Mazzone's similar use of him at Brescia. The segment concludes with a newspaper clipping about Andriy Shevchenko’s knee injury, indicating a 45-day stoppage with a photo of Shevchenko in a Milan jersey, as the narrator mentions his injury at the beginning of the season. Throughout, a consistent, slightly dramatic instrumental background music plays.

The segment opens with a dramatic, suspenseful orchestral score as a male narrator introduces the scene. Visually, Carlo Ancelotti in a suit is featured in a montage with AC Milan players like Andrea Pirlo in their iconic red-and-black jerseys, alongside a Deportivo player in blue-and-white, all against a dark red grid with the AC Milan crest. The narrator explains that in the Champions League group stages, Milan traveled to face "the tough opponent Deportivo La Coruña at the Riazor Stadium." The scene transitions to a tactical formation graphic, displaying Ancelotti's "favorite formation," the 4-3-2-1, with player names like Kaladze, Maldini, Nesta, Simic, Gattuso, Pirlo, Seedorf, Rui Costa, Rivaldo, and Inzaghi. The narrator specifies that "the three roaming duo of Rui Costa and Rivaldo would play behind the sole striker, which in that case was Inzaghi." Real match footage from the 2002/03 Champions League then shows Milan's 0-4 victory over Deportivo on September 24, 2002, with players celebrating goals, as the narrator states, "the Rossoneri won by four goals to nil," making them "one of the favorites for the title." Despite "inconsistent form in the league and Shevchenko's slow return from injury," Milan managed to reach the Champions League final. Highlights from the quarter-finals against Ajax (Milan 3-2, April 23, 2003) and the semi-finals against Inter (Inter 1-1 Milan, May 13, 2003) are shown, capturing emotional intensity. During the semi-final footage, Shevchenko scores in stoppage time, accompanied by excited crowd cheering and a commentator exclaiming, "Shevchenko! Shevchenko!" The narrator then sets up the final, stating Milan "will face Juve in a Champions League final with an Italian taste," highlighting "several headlines for the match: Ancelotti against his old employer and Del Piero against his." The segment concludes with Ancelotti in a press conference, followed by a new formation graphic for Juventus (4-4-2) featuring players like Buffon, Zambrotta, Del Piero, and Trezeguet, with the Juventus crest, underscoring Ancelotti's broader managerial journey.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with a tactical formation graphic contrasting Juventus' 4-4-2 with AC Milan's 4-3-1-2, featuring Carlo Ancelotti and Fatih Terim, as the narrator discusses Ancelotti's tactical adjustments. The focus shifts to the 2003 Champions League Final, a scoreless draw against Juventus, leading to a penalty shootout where Johan Cruyff's critique of shootouts as 'not football' is mentioned. AC Milan ultimately wins the shootout 5-2, with Dida making key saves and Shevchenko converting the decisive penalty, prompting an Italian commentator to exclaim 'Rete! Rete! Il Milan è campione d'Europa!' as players celebrate lifting the Champions League Trophy. A subsequent scene shows the 2003 Coppa Italia Final victory over Roma, with an Italian commentator announcing 'Ed eccola la Coppa alzata da Paolo Maldini, capitano del Milan!' as the Coppa Italia Trophy is raised. The narrative concludes with a montage of Milan’s 2003 successes, highlighting Leonardo's acquisition of Kaka and praising the management's squad strengthening strategy.

raw_segments: The video opens with a tactical formation graphic comparing Juventus' 4-4-2 and AC Milan's 4-3-1-2, overlaid with images of managers Carlo Ancelotti and Fatih Terim, as a narrator discusses tactical approaches, mentioning Ancelotti changing shape. The scene transitions to the 2003 Champions League Final between Juventus and Milan, a tense match ending 0-0 after 90 minutes, with the narrator quoting Johan Cruyff's criticism that a penalty shootout is 'not football.' A dramatic penalty shootout follows, where Milan triumphs 5-2, with the narrator detailing Dida's crucial saves and Shevchenko's successful penalty. The celebration of Milan’s victory is shown with players lifting the Champions League trophy, accompanied by an excited Italian commentator shouting 'Rete! Rete! Il Milan è campione d'Europa!' A quick cut then shows the 2003 Coppa Italia Final, where Milan beats Roma, with another Italian commentator exclaiming 'Ed eccola la Coppa alzata da Paolo Maldini, capitano del Milan!' as the Coppa Italia Trophy is held aloft. The atmosphere shifts to a montage of Milan’s 2003 triumphs, including both trophies, as the narrator highlights Leonardo’s foresight in acquiring Kaka, praising the 'young Brazilian' and emphasizing a clever management team strengthening the squad.

event_blocks.summary: Journalist Andrea Massala relayed Leonardo's praise for Kaka, describing him as a player around whom AC Milan could build, a sentiment underscored by a voiceover stating Galiani "didn't hesitate" to sign the "young Brazilian." Kaka's arrival at AC Milan was marked by a visual of his €8.50M transfer fee, followed by Cafu, identified as the "national team captain" and acquired for 'FREE', who was "recommended by the doctor Merman." Kaka was then depicted smiling in a suit upon his arrival at the club, proudly displaying his new Milan number 22 jersey. The voiceover emphasized Kaka's immediate impact, stating he "quickly proved that he wasn't only the future, but the present as well," effectively replacing Rivaldo. Tactical graphics illustrated Kaka's integration into Ancelotti's 4-3-2-1 and 4-3-1-2 formations, highlighting his "freedom to roam" and "great link-up play" with teammates such as Shevchenko, Inzaghi, and Tomasson. Match highlights showcased Kaka scoring against Roma in a 1-0 victory at the San Siro on May 2, 2004, a "crucial win against rivals Roma" that contributed to Ancelotti securing the league title. Further highlights featured Kaka scoring in a Champions League quarter-final against Deportivo on March 23, 2004, culminating in a goal celebration with teammates, amidst which an excited commentator exclaimed "Shevchenko!" amid crowd cheers. The narrative concluded by referencing a "European trip" that ended with a "historic remonta."

raw_segments: The video opens with a quote from Leonardo, relayed by journalist Andrea Massala, praising Kaka as a player on whom they can build something, set against a red grid with Kaka in a yellow Brazil jersey. A voiceover explains that Galiani "didn't hesitate" to sign the "young Brazilian" immediately. The scene transitions to Kaka in an AC Milan jersey, with his €8.50M transfer fee displayed, followed by Cafu, the "national team captain," in a white Milan jersey, labeled 'FREE', with the voiceover noting he was "recommended by the doctor Merman." Kaka is then seen arriving at the club, smiling in a suit, and proudly holding up his new Milan number 22 jersey. The voiceover continues, stating Kaka "quickly proved that he wasn't only the future, but the present as well," taking Rivaldo's place. A montage shows Kaka in action, celebrating a goal, with tactical graphics illustrating his role in Ancelotti's 4-3-2-1 and 4-3-1-2 formations, highlighting his "freedom to roam" and "great link-up play" with teammates like Shevchenko, Inzaghi, and Tomasson. Match highlights follow, with Kaka scoring against Roma in a 1-0 win on May 2, 2004, a "crucial win against rivals Roma at the San Siro" that helped Ancelotti win the league title. Another highlight shows Kaka scoring in a Champions League quarter-final against Deportivo on March 23, 2004, with the ball entering the net and Kaka celebrating with teammates. During a goal celebration, an excited commentator shouts "Shevchenko!" amidst crowd cheers, as the voiceover concludes by mentioning a "European trip" ending with a "historic remonta."

event_blocks.summary: AC Milan experienced a significant setback in the 2003-04 Champions League quarter-finals, suffering a historic 4-5 penalty shootout defeat to Deportivo La Coruña after a 4-4 aggregate draw, a result Carlo Ancelotti described as inexplicable, as indicated by a newspaper headline overlay. The narrative then shifts to a montage celebrating Milan's 2003-04 season triumphs, including the league title and Coppa Italia, highlighting key players like Kaka, Shevchenko, and Inzaghi, and the stable management of Silvio Berlusconi and Adriano Galliani, with Kaka's development and new transfers noted as crucial for future improvement. This celebratory tone, marked by confetti and trophy lifts, is juxtaposed with the preceding despair. Concurrently, Liverpool's search for a manager in March 2004 is detailed, with Chief Executive Rick Parry observing José Mourinho's Porto defeat Manchester United 2-1 in the UEFA Cup, a match that sparked Mourinho's interest in managing Liverpool. Despite initial impressions of Mourinho's tactical acumen, his extravagant celebrations and perceived character concerns led Parry and chairman David Moores to appoint Rafael Benítez, who had achieved significant success with Valencia, including two La Liga titles and the UEFA Cup. Benítez is depicted in press conferences and training sessions, tasked with a challenging role at Liverpool, building on the club's 2001 UEFA Cup victory.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a dramatic Champions League quarter-final match, as a male narrator describes AC Milan suffering a "huge defeat" after Deportivo La Coruña's "historic comeback." Visually, a goal is scored by Milan, but the tension builds to a penalty shootout where Deportivo triumphs 4-5 after a 4-4 draw, confirmed by the on-screen text '03/04 CHAMPIONS LEAGUE - QTR-FINALS DEPORTIVO 4(5-4)0 MILAN FULL TIME'. A loud crowd cheers erupts, followed by a sustained roar, underscoring Deportivo's victory. The narrator calls this "a huge surprise," as the scene shifts to a dejected Carlo Ancelotti, Milan’s manager, with a black-and-white newspaper clipping overlay reading 'Everything went wrong: it's inexplicable' and 'Hanno sbagliato tutti', emphasizing the team’s collapse. After a brief silence, a low, ominous synth hum begins, building into a deep, resonant orchestral score as the narrative transitions to a celebratory montage of Milan’s 2003-04 season. A 'ROAD TO FINAL' title card appears, followed by a graphic showing the 04-05 Champions League final between Liverpool and AC Milan. The narrator explains that "Milan and Liverpool entered the season in completely different circumstances," highlighting that "The Rossoneri won the league title and the Champions League in the last two years." The visuals showcase Milan’s triumphs, including lifting the Coppa Italia trophy, and feature key players like Kaka (number 22, celebrating goals), Shevchenko (celebrating a semi-final goal), and Inzaghi. Club officials Silvio Berlusconi and Adriano Galliani are also seen, while a tactical formation graphic illustrates Kaka’s central role. The narrator continues, noting Milan is "supported by a stable management team" with a "complete squad with room for improvement, depending on Kaka's development as a player and their new transfers." The tone shifts between despair and triumph, culminating in a montage of Milan’s 2003 success, including the Champions League and Coppa Italia victories, with confetti, emotional embraces, and trophy lifts, all set against the dramatic orchestral music. The final frames show a red grid background with the AC Milan crest, as the narrator's sentence about "the Reds" is cut short.

The segment opens with a split-screen showing a Liverpool player in a red 'Carlsberg' jersey looking distressed with hands on his head, juxtaposed with a man in a suit walking on a football pitch, as a calm male narrator discusses the club's search for a new manager in March 2004. The scene transitions to a football match between Manchester United and Porto, with score overlays indicating a progression from 0-0 to 1-1, and finally 1-2, as players in blue and white kits celebrate a goal. Amidst excited football commentary shouting "It's been turned in by Costinha!" and crowd cheering, the narrator explains that Liverpool's Chief Executive Rick Parry observed Porto's triumph, led by José Mourinho, at Old Trafford. A close-up of José Mourinho, a man with dark hair in a black coat, appears alongside a quote from Rick Parry, stating Mourinho's interest in managing Liverpool. The narrator notes that while management was impressed, Mourinho's extravagant celebration created a negative impression. Mourinho is then seen walking on the pitch, followed by another quote from Parry questioning if Mourinho’s character aligned with Liverpool’s values. The narrator continues, explaining that Parry and chairman David Moores ultimately decided to appoint the Spanish manager Rafa Benítez, detailing his prior success winning La Liga twice and the UEFA Cup with Valencia. The narrative then focuses on Rafael Benítez, a man in a dark suit with a red tie, shown in a press conference and leading training sessions, giving instructions to players. The video highlights Liverpool’s UEFA Cup victory in 2001, with players celebrating on the podium, holding the trophy and wearing medals. Benítez is again seen serious and focused in a press conference, followed by a montage of his tenure, including training sessions and a celebratory moment with players, emphasizing his pivotal role in Liverpool’s success during this period, as the narrator concludes that the Spaniard faced a very tough job.

event_blocks.summary: Newspaper clippings hinted at Steven Gerrard's near departure to Chelsea and Liverpool's financial difficulties, with a narrator confirming Gerrard's close call and Michael Owen's consideration of Real Madrid, while the club itself was reportedly for sale. Amidst management's assurance of time for Benitez's project, a man in a red training top appeared on a training ground, followed by Liverpool players at a press conference. The departure of Michael Owen to Real Madrid was visually represented by a bald man in a white kit holding a ball, occurring after Gerrard's decision to stay. Liverpool's new signings were then introduced: Djibril Cissé, Xabi Alonso, and Luis García, identified as the 'Spanish duo' and the 'French striker.' AC Milan's acquisitions followed, with a graphic showing Jaap Stam, Hernán Crespo, and Pancaro, as the narrator detailed the arrival of the 'tough defender Jaap Stam' and 'the Argentine striker Crespo on loan from Chelsea.' Gérard Houllier appeared with a serious expression before a split-screen showed a Liverpool vs. Deportivo match, with the narrator explaining Liverpool's need to win a tough qualifier against 'the Austrian side Grazer' for Champions League group stage entry. Champions League Group A (Deportivo, Liverpool, Monaco, Olympiacos) and Group F (Barcelona, Milan, Celtic, Shakhtar) graphics were displayed, with Fatih Terim appearing beside Group F as the narrator noted Milan's challenging group. The segment concluded with AC Milan vs. Barcelona highlights, featuring a goal and celebration. Subsequently, AC Milan, 'The Rossoneri,' won their group, leading Group F standings. In contrast, Liverpool faced a critical situation in Group A, needing a two-goal victory against Olympiacos at Anfield. During the Liverpool vs. Olympiacos match, Rivaldo scored for Olympiacos in the 27th minute, making it 0-1 amidst crowd cheers and a suspenseful ticking sound. Liverpool equalized in the 47th minute through Sinama-Pongolle, who had an immediate impact after being substituted on, leading to loud cheers. Mellor then scored in the 80th minute, putting Liverpool ahead 2-1 as the crowd erupted. The match concluded with Liverpool winning 2-1, the scoreboard showing 85:36, accompanied by the persistent ticking sound.

event_blocks.summary: The minute opens with a wide shot of a football match where Liverpool, in red, faces Olympiacos in black-and-white, with the scoreboard displaying '04/05 CHAMPIONS LEAGUE - GROUP A LIVERPOOL 3-1 OLYMPIACOS GERRARD - 86''. Amidst crowd roars and excited commentary exclaiming "Gerrard! Oh, you beauty! What a hit, what a hit!", the scene cuts to a close-up of Steven Gerrard celebrating his powerful goal with arms raised, followed by a replay. The narrative then shifts to a montage of Andriy Shevchenko in an AC Milan jersey celebrating a goal, as a narrator explains his "exceptional performances" led to him being handed the "prestigious Ballon d'Or in December," visually confirmed by a 2004 Ballon d'Or Graphic showing Shevchenko holding the trophy with vote totals. The video transitions to a red grid background with images of Rafael Benítez and Liverpool players, highlighting '17 EPL GOALS' and poor Match Results Graphic like '2-8' and '8-1'. The narrator states that "On the flip side, Rafa Benítez was struggling to adjust to the Premier League," with the team suffering "many defeats," including a "catastrophic defeat against relegation-fighting Southampton." Benítez is shown distressed, while a quote from ex-legend Alan Hansen appears: 'THE WORST PERFORMANCE SINCE MY RETIREMENT', attributed by the narrator to Hansen's description of the defeat. A black-and-white Newspaper Headlines clipping with 'Gerrard gives warning on leaving of Liverpool' then appears, followed by a quote from Steven Gerrard: 'HE DOESN'T RATE ME AS MUCH AS I THINK HE SHOULD RATE ME'. The narrator confirms that "rumors linking Gerrard to Chelsea were back" and Gerrard "mentioned recently in a documentary that Benítez didn't rate him as much as he thought he should rate him." The final scenes show Benítez gesturing intensely during a match with an 'fa.com' banner visible, as the narrator concludes by mentioning "The Spaniard's failure of building a personal relationship with Gerrard could eventually push the English..."

event_blocks.summary: Rafael Benítez, Liverpool’s manager, passionately gestured on the sidelines as the narrator detailed his success in steering the team through knockout competitions. A football match ensued, featuring a Liverpool player in red and a Chelsea player in blue vying for possession, with a 'Sky Sports' advertisement visible. The narrative highlighted Liverpool's path to the League Cup final against Chelsea, who were aggressively pursuing their captain, Steven Gerrard. Vibrant Liverpool fans displayed a 'YOU’LL NEVER WALK ALONE' banner, contrasted by Chelsea fans with their blue crest banner. The '2005 LEAGUE CUP FINAL LIVERPOOL - CHELSEA FEB 27, 2005' graphic appeared. During the match, after a Liverpool player scored, Steven Gerrard ironically scored a crucial own goal, confirmed by the scoreboard showing 'LIVERPOOL 1-1 CHELSEA GERRARD (OG) - 79’'. An excited commentator exclaimed, "Chelsea a goal! Steven Gerrard of all people! Mr. Liverpool gives Chelsea the equalizing goal!" The narrator then noted that crucial Champions League spots were unattainable for Liverpool. The narrator explained that "The Rossoneri" (Milan) maintained their Serie A lead until a "fateful match against Juve at the San Siro," where both teams were level on points. A split-screen graphic showed Milan and Juventus players with '76'. The final scene depicted a Milan versus Juventus match, where a Juventus player, David Trézéguet, scored a goal following "a beautiful assist by Del Piero," as indicated by the on-screen text '04/05 SERIE A - GW35 MILAN 0-1 JUVENTUS DAVID TRÉZÉGUE - 28’'.

The narrator elaborates that 'the Reds' took revenge against Bayer Leverkusen in the round of 16, producing an unexpected attacking display to win by six goals to two after two legs. event_blocks.summary: The narrative synthesizes two distinct Champions League quarter-final first-leg matches from 2005. Initially, Liverpool establishes a commanding 2-0 lead against Juventus, with Sami Hyypiä scoring a header in the 10th minute and Luis García adding a second in the 25th minute, despite commentary mentioning "Ibrahimovic against the post" and a "good save" for Del Piero. The scene then shifts to a Milan derby, where AC Milan takes a 1-0 lead through Stam in first-half stoppage time, followed by Shevchenko extending their advantage to 2-0 in the 74th minute. The second leg begins with Juventus pulling a goal back to make it 2-1 (Cannavaro, 63'), but Liverpool ultimately wins the tie. The focus then returns to the AC Milan vs. Inter Milan second leg, where Shevchenko scores in the 30th minute to make it 1-0 to Milan (3-0 on aggregate), and later adds a second goal, described as a "strike of staggering power," to secure a 2-0 victory for Milan (4-0 aggregate), with commentary also noting missed chances by Baroš, Cruz, and Martins.

The scene quickly shifts to a wide-angle view of a football match between Liverpool in red and Juventus in black-and-white stripes. This is swiftly followed by a second goal from Luis García, with the commentary exclaiming "Luis Garcia! What an effort! What a goal!", as the text updates to 'LIVERPOOL 2-0 JUVENTUS LUIS GARCÍA - 25’'. A dejected Juventus player is then shown, followed by a concerned man in a dark suit and glasses. English commentary also mentions "Ibrahimovic against the post" and "Del Piero, good save!" during this segment, possibly referring to other match events, while Italian commentary reinforces Dida's presence with "Dida ancora!". The video transitions to a different match, AC Milan in red-and-black stripes against Inter in blue. Italian commentary announces "Pirlo per Stam! Yaps! 1-0!" as Stam scores, leading to a loud crowd roar and the on-screen text '2005 CHAMPIONS LEAGUE - QTR-FINALS - 1ST LEG MILAN 1-0 INTER STAM - 45+1’'. English commentary briefly mentions "Baros! Another chance to make it three!" likely referring back to the Liverpool match. The action continues with a second goal for Milan, with Italian commentary stating "E c'è il 2-0! Shevchenko!", as the text confirms 'MILAN 2-0 INTER SHEVCHENKO - 74’', again met with a loud crowd roar. The segment concludes with a wide shot of the pitch, emphasizing the intensity of the match.

The minute opens with the roar of the crowd as an English commentator exclaims, "He's missed it! Juventus have pulled one back!" We see a Juventus player in a black-and-white striped jersey celebrating a goal, while a Liverpool goalkeeper in a green jersey reacts with frustration. On-screen text confirms 'LIVERPOOL 2-1 JUVENTUS' with 'CANNAVARO - 63'' indicating the scorer and time. The scene transitions to a dejected Liverpool player in a red jersey and a wide shot of the pitch, followed by the English commentator declaring, "It's all over at Anfield! Liverpool have beaten Juventus!" amidst continued crowd cheers. A title card announces '2ND LEG' as Liverpool players in red jerseys walk onto the pitch. An Italian commentator excitedly calls, "Seedorf trova ancora Shevchenko, sinistro!" as a player in a red-and-black striped jersey scores a goal, confirmed by on-screen text: '2005 CHAMPIONS LEAGUE - QTR-FINALS - 2ND LEG INTER 0(0-3)1 MILAN SHEVCHENKO - 30''. The player, identified as Shevchenko, celebrates with a raised arm and clenched fist. The Italian commentator adds, "2-0 per il Milan!" as the crowd roars. Later, an English commentator describes a missed opportunity by Ibrahimovic, followed by another strong shot. The action returns to Shevchenko, with the English commentator exclaiming, "This is Shevchenko! GOAL! A strike of staggering power!" as we see a goalkeeper in a green jersey diving in vain as the ball enters the net. The sequence concludes with a close-up of Shevchenko with a focused expression, and a final wide shot of the ball entering the net, while further commentary notes missed chances by Baroš and play involving Cruz and Martins.

event_blocks.summary: In a 2005 Champions League quarter-final second leg, a Juventus player in a black-and-white striped jersey struck the post, with the rebound scrambled away by Liverpool goalkeeper Dudek for a corner; the match concluded 0-0, with Liverpool advancing 2-1 on aggregate. Simultaneously, in another quarter-final, an AC Milan player in a red-and-black striped jersey scored, leading to a 3-0 victory over Inter Milan and the match being abandoned amidst chaos. Later, José Mourinho, identified as Chelsea Manager, spoke at a press conference, referencing "a team with one of the best managers in the world and some of the best players in this country." Earlier in the tournament, a montage featured commentary on Liverpool, described as "primed by Rafa Benitez" and "not in the habit of losing," and PSV, with manager Guus Hiddink noting them as "one of the best organized defensive teams in the world." An AC Milan player discussed creating chances "through football." In a separate match, commentary highlighted Chelsea players "Arshavin," "Petr Cech" with a fingertip save, "Farfan," "Joe Cole," "Lampard," "Drogba," and "Gudjohnsen." The first leg of a semi-final saw AC Milan defeat PSV 1-0, with Andriy Shevchenko scoring in the 42nd minute after build-up play involving Andrea Pirlo and Kaká, as confirmed by the title card '2005 CHAMPIONS LEAGUE - SEMIFINALS - 1ST LEG MILAN 1-0 PSV SHEVCHENKO - 42''. Reactions from Liverpool and Chelsea players, including mentions of Luis García and Steven Gerrard, concluded the segment.

Following a wide-angle view and a close-up of an intense player in a red jersey, the commentary highlighted 'Chance here, Riise,' with 'Cech saves it.' The atmosphere escalated with loud cheers as a player in a red-and-black striped jersey celebrated a goal, confirmed by an on-screen title card: '2005 CHAMPIONS LEAGUE - SEMIFINALS - 1ST LEG MILAN 2-0 PSV TOMASSON - 90',' prompting the commentator to exclaim, 'And that makes all the difference!' The narrative shifted to the news that 'Xabi Alonso is out of the second leg,' concluding that 'Liverpool have got a goalless draw in the first leg of the semi-final.' A player in a red jersey gestured in frustration as a title card displayed '2005 CHAMPIONS LEAGUE - SEMIFINALS - 1ST LEG CHELSEA 0-0 LIVERPOOL FULL TIME.' The end of the match showed a dejected player in a red jersey, a player in a blue jersey celebrating, and a player in a red jersey with the quote 'A TEAM WITH A GLORIOUS PAST THAT EVERYBODY RESPECTS ALL OVER THE WORLD.' Dramatic orchestral music preceded a press conference where José Mourinho, identified as 'CHELSEA MANAGER,' stated with a Portuguese accent, 'I think we can score goals. I think we can get results. I think it's a complete different game for them. One thing is to come and play for a 0-0, another thing is to play and get a result. And I'm very confident we are going to the final.' This was followed by a player in a blue jersey with the quote 'WHAT WE TRY TO DO IS CREATE OUR CHANCES THROUGH THE FOOTBALL,' and a final statement in Portuguese from another speaker: 'É uma ótima equipa, uma equipa organizada, penso que quer ir à final.'

event_blocks.summary: The Champions League match setup in Eindhoven, featuring the iconic logo and banners for Liverpool and AC Milan, commenced with commentary noting the pressure. Close-ups of passionate fans waving flags and banners were interspersed with commentary highlighting the financial disparity between teams, contrasting an expensive squad with the strength of supporters, a good manager, good players, and balance. The scene shifted to Anfield for a significant night of English and European football, with a declaration of intent to provide a hard challenge. On-field action began with Liverpool players in red 'Carlsberg' jerseys against Chelsea in blue, accompanied by a rising crowd roar. Luis García scored in the 4th minute, with commentary noting the goal and the score overlay showing Liverpool 1-0 Chelsea, followed by crowd cheers. Subsequently, in a PSV vs. AC Milan match, Park Ji-sung, in a red-and-white PSV jersey, scored in the 9th minute, prompting commentary of "Park is there again to finish it!" PSV fans in red-and-white checkered scarves celebrated wildly with audible cheers, and the score overlay updated to PSV 1-0 Milan. Close-ups of a manager in a dark suit reacting intensely and players like Lampard and Makelele underscored the high stakes and emotional depth of these Champions League knockout matches, with dynamic camera movements capturing the electric atmosphere.

raw_segments: The segment opens with an aerial view of a grand Champions League match setup, featuring the iconic logo at the center of the pitch, flanked by banners for Liverpool and AC Milan, as commentary welcomes viewers to Eindhoven, noting that "the pressure is on." This transitions to intense close-ups of passionate fans waving flags and banners, while a male voiceover highlights the financial disparity, stating, "They have the most expensive team in the world," but emphasizing Liverpool's strength: "but we have our supporters, good manager, good players, and we have the balance now." Commentary then shifts, welcoming viewers to Anfield for a "massive night for English and yes, European football," as another determined male voice declares, "We are not surrendering that easy. Let's go and give them another hard time." On-field action begins with Liverpool players in red 'Carlsberg' jerseys against Chelsea in blue, accompanied by a rising crowd roar. Intense commentary follows as Luis García scores in the 4th minute, with the commentator exclaiming, "Luis Garcia is there as well! That might well have crossed the line anyway! He's been given! Liverpool are ahead! What a start!" The crowd erupts in cheers as the score overlay shows Liverpool 1-0 Chelsea. The scene then cuts to PSV vs. AC Milan, where Park Ji-sung, in a red-and-white PSV jersey, scores in the 9th minute, prompting the commentator to declare, "Park is there again to finish it!" PSV fans in red-and-white checkered scarves celebrate wildly, their cheers clearly audible, as the score overlay updates to PSV 1-0 Milan. Close-ups of a manager in a dark suit reacting intensely and players like Lampard and Makelele underscore the high stakes and emotional depth of these Champions League knockout matches, with dynamic camera movements capturing the electric atmosphere.

event_blocks.summary: The match commenced with rapid exchanges, featuring Benegor Hesling's header striking the crossbar and a near goal from Garcia and Lampard, thwarted by a wonderful save by Dudek, followed by a mistake by Ricardo Carvalho that led to a Cech shot. At the 65th minute, a PSV player, identified as Cocu, scored with a header past the diving goalkeeper, igniting audible crowd cheers and making the aggregate score 2-2. As the 89:50 mark approached, a Milan player displayed frustration, and the commentator noted the intense tension. In the 90+1 minute, Ambrosini dramatically scored for Milan, prompting the commentator to exclaim, "Ambrosini! When it matters, Milan deliver!", as the crowd erupted again, shifting the aggregate score to 2-3. However, PSV quickly countered, with Cocu scoring again at 90+2', updating the scoreboard to 'PSV 3(3-3)1 MILAN'. Simultaneously, a rapid sequence unfolded where Dudek failed to secure the ball properly after a play involving Terry and Kaiman, with Johnson scoring, leading to commentator disbelief: "Oh my goodness me, can you believe that! Right at the death!". The goalkeeper reacted in despair, and the manager was restrained by security amidst the electric atmosphere. The full-time score concluded as PSV 3(3-3)1 MILAN.

event_blocks.summary: AC Milan players, clad in red training tops and black shorts, engaged in warm-ups and stretching on a verdant pitch, underscored by a dramatic orchestral score, as a narrator emphasized Milan's 13 Champions League titles against Liverpool's inaugural final appearance. Carlo Ancelotti's confident assertion, 'we are less nervous than the final a couple of years ago against Juve... there are no unknowns and we are convinced of winning,' was highlighted, contrasting with Xabi Alonso's acknowledgment, 'it's true, Milan are the favorites, but anything can happen in a final,' shown with a close-up of the smiling man in a red jacket. The narrator noted Liverpool's defensive strength, having not conceded in their last three European matches, before a scoreboard flashed 'LIVERPOOL 1 - 0 MILAN' at 45:00, followed by a tense sideline exchange between Rafael Benítez and Carlo Ancelotti. Tactical graphics revealed Milan's 4-3-2-1 formation featuring Shevchenko, Kaka, and Pirlo, and Liverpool's 4-4-2 with Gerrard, Alonso, and Dudek. The narrator detailed Ancelotti's strategic addition of striker Shevchenko, reinforcing Milan's 'overwhelming favorites' status, while Rafa Benítez's gamble with Harry Kewell starting alongside Barosh was mentioned. The segment concluded with a smiling Liverpool player in a black training top on the pitch, as the orchestral music swelled.

Another commentator highlights the "showpiece final" featuring "two of the greatest names in European Cup history," noting that "every player in the world wants to be tonight." A wide aerial shot captures a massive, vibrant crowd of red and white-clad fans marching towards the stadium. AC Milan players, including Seedorf (number 20) and Kaka (number 22), walk onto the pitch in white jerseys with red trim and 'OPEL' sponsorship, followed by Liverpool players in red training tops. The teams line up for the pre-match ceremony under a smoky, dramatic sky, with the Champions League Logo displayed. The match commences with intense action, including a Liverpool Player in Red sliding to tackle. Suddenly, an Italian commentator excitedly shouts, "Parte Pirlo, un mazzo, al palo, rete! Rete! Il Milan in vantaggio!" as Maldini scores Milan's opening goal. Another commentator exclaims, "A goal in 50 seconds for Milan! Oh, would you believe it! Paolo Maldini, the skipper, has scored it!" Milan players celebrate enthusiastically, Maldini raising his arms in triumph, and the Scoreboard Display confirms Milan's 1-0 lead at 1:44. The atmosphere remains electric with fans waving flags and banners amidst stadium smoke. Liverpool responds with a goal, a player in red celebrating joyfully with teammates, and the scoreboard updates to 1-1. The match continues with dynamic play, including a corner kick and a shot on goal, with the Liverpool goalkeeper diving to save, as a commentator describes a play "Swung in towards Hyypiä! event_blocks.summary: The match began with AC Milan dominating, leading 2-0 with goals from Hernán Crespo, who scored in the 39th and 44th minutes, and Maldini, as indicated by the scoreboard and ecstatic Italian commentary. Milan coach Carlo Ancelotti was animated on the sidelines, while Liverpool manager Rafael Benítez appeared frustrated. Liverpool fans were in despair, with some covering their faces, as Milan players in white jerseys controlled possession. The first half concluded with Milan leading 3-0, with Kaka noted for an assist. The second half saw a dramatic shift: Steven Gerrard scored for Liverpool in the 54th minute, narrowing the deficit to 3-1, sparking fervent celebration among Liverpool fans. Vladimir Smicer quickly followed with another goal in the 56th minute, making it 3-2 and prompting commentary about Liverpool's comeback. The tension peaked when Xabi Alonso scored the equalizer in the 60th minute, hitting the back of the net after a penalty save by Milan's goalkeeper Dida, bringing the score to 3-3. This remarkable turnaround, with Liverpool scoring three goals in six minutes after being 3-0 down, left Liverpool players in disbelief and joy, contrasting sharply with the shocked and despairing Milan fans and bench, including Carlo Ancelotti.

The camera captures the Milan coach, Carlo Ancelotti, animatedly gesturing on the sidelines, while Liverpool’s manager, Rafael Benítez, appears frustrated, shouting with his hands raised, against the backdrop of ongoing English commentary discussing a play involving Luis Garcia and Maldini. The double of Milan!). The crowd of Liverpool fans, dressed in red, is shown in a state of despair, some covering their faces, as the English commentator remarks 'look at that from Kaka', likely referring to the assist. The video then cuts to a close-up of a downcast Steven Gerrard, followed by a shot of the scoreboard displaying '43:57' and '3-0', with Maldini, Crespo, and Crespo listed as scorers, while the Italian commentator continues his ecstatic praise, exclaiming 'Straordinario Kaka in verticale per Crespo! Crespo! Il tiro! Rete! Rete! Rete! Il terzo gol del Milan! C'è una squadra sola in campo!' (Extraordinary Kaka vertically for Crespo! Crespo! The shot! Net! Net! Net! The third goal of Milan! There is only one team on the field!). The final frames show a wide shot of the pitch and a close-up of a defeated Liverpool player, as the Italian commentator continues to exclaim 'Straordinario!' (Extraordinary!), and the English commentator grimly concludes, stating 'Milan now playing football out of this world. Nobody can live with this,' and that 'Liverpool are all but beaten before half-time.'

The video opens with a wide shot of a football match, showing Liverpool players in red and AC Milan players in white on the pitch, as a commentator describes the "last action of a half that Milan dominated from beginning to end," highlighting Crespo's two goals and Paolo Maldini giving Milan an "unlikely lead." The action returns to the pitch where a Liverpool player takes an "ambitious" shot that is "well struck" but "wasn't far away." The AC Milan goalkeeper makes a "good save" from what the commentator identifies as "Shevchenko." The ball is then "charged down by Kaka" before the commentator exclaims, "It was Gerrard!" as Gerrard is shown running with a determined expression, coinciding with the on-screen text 'MILAN 3-1 LIVERPOOL GERRARD - 54'' and a roaring crowd, confirming his goal.

Steven Gerrard!" as the scoreboard confirms 'MILAN 3-1 LIVERPOOL GERRARD - 54''. Steven Gerrard, seen celebrating with arms raised, has just scored, narrowing the deficit from Milan's earlier goals by Maldini and Crespo. The commentator adds, "Hold on, this could be some ride. You never know, you never know in Liverpool." The camera then pans to the stunned and dejected Milan fans. The excitement intensifies as Vladimir Smicer scores, with the commentator shouting, "Hit by Smicer! He did! It's in! Vladimir Smicer!" The crowd's roar swells as the graphic updates to 'MILAN 3-2 LIVERPOOL ŠMÍCER - 56''. The commentator highlights, "Two goals in two minutes for Liverpool!" and reflects, "Liverpool were a beaten team at the interval, and now look." The tension peaks as the commentator describes a penalty incident involving Gerrard, then exclaims, "Oh, saved by Dida! Xabi Alonso!" as Xabi Alonso scores the equalizer in the 60th minute, the ball hitting the back of the net after the initial penalty save. The scoreboard flashes 'MILAN 3-3 LIVERPOOL ALONSO - 60'', and the commentator declares, "An mission impossible is accomplished!" Liverpool fans are in wild celebration, their cheers echoing, while Milan's bench, including Carlo Ancelotti, looks on with concern. The scoreboard at 60:29 confirms the 3-3 tie, as Liverpool players embrace in disbelief and joy, contrasting sharply with the shocked and despairing Milan fans and a close-up of a dejected Milan player. The commentator concludes, "Liverpool were 3-0 down five minutes ago... And now look at that scoreline. Dida has been beaten three times."

event_blocks.summary: The match reached a dramatic 3-3 halftime score, with Liverpool players like Gerrard and Osnos displaying visible distress after a period of intense play where a shot was described as 'sul la linea!' and Jimmy Traore made a crucial save, followed by Jamie Carragher's vital tackle. The second half saw the Milan Goalkeeper make a spectacular save against a Liverpool player, but Jerzy Dudek then made a remarkable double save against Shevchenko, much to the shock of the Liverpool crowd. The game concluded in a 3-3 draw, leading to a penalty shootout. In the shootout, Dudek saved Serginho's and Pirlo's penalties, while Milan's goalkeeper Dida saved Smicer's shot. However, Liverpool's Harman, Cissé, and a final unnamed player scored their penalties, alongside Milan's Tomasson. Despite Crespo and Shevchenko scoring for Milan, and Gerrard and Alonso for Liverpool, the final penalty taken by a Liverpool player secured their victory. The segment concludes with the commentator declaring Liverpool champions of Europe, amidst chaotic celebrations with fans, confetti, and Steven Gerrard hoisting the Champions League Trophy, before transitioning to credits and a 'balón' title card.
